channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - kotaku.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for kotaku.com

item.title: The Week In Games: Finally Going Hands-On With ‘Staggering’ Open-World Showpiece Crimson Desert
item.content:encoded:
Crimson Desert has had one of the more unusual marketing rollouts in recent years. It was announced back in 2019 and no one really paid attention to it until it made a splash at Gamescom Opening Night Live 2023 when the open-world game showed off horse drifting. Ever since, there’s been hype around its big ambitions and visual spectacle, and skepticism around, like, what you actually spend most of your time doing in the game and whether it’s actually any fun. This week we’ll finally get to see if it can deliver.

Digital Foundry has had glowing coverage of its early access to the game, including a PS5 Pro version that was shared with the expert analysts there via a dedicated console that had the game pre-installed, presumably so they couldn’t also test the base PS5 version that most people will be playing. But whether the grand spectacle can be achieved with less hardware horsepower is only part of the equation. I’m equally curious whether the open world feels immersive and alive like those in Zelda: Breath of the Wild and Ghost of Yotei, or if it’s more of a checklist of map icons like in some Ubisoft games.

I’ll be picking up my physical copy this Thursday from the store when it launches . Unfortunately, it requires a massive day-one download right out of the box. Is that why console versions weren’t available to reviewers ahead of release? In the meantime, my plate’s still full with Slay the Spire 2, Marathon, and last week’s Monster Hunter Stories 3, a surprisingly impressive turn-based action-adventure I’m using to try to JRPG-pill my kids.

Monday, March 16

The Seven Deadly Sins: Origin | PS5, PC

Starship Troopers: Ultimate Bug War! | PS5, Xbox Series X/S, Switch 2, PC

Grind Survivors | PC

Deadline Delivery | PC

Tuesday, March 17

Dread Delusion (port) | PS5, PS4, Xbox Series X/S, Xbox One, Switch 2

MLB The Show 26 | PS5, Xbox Series X/S, PC

Thomas & Friends: Wonders of Sodor | PS5, PS4, Xbox Series X/S, Xbox One, Switch, PC

Deadzone: Rogue | Switch 2

Everwind | PC

Fallen Tear: The Ascension | PC

Wednesday, March 18

Shadow Tactics: Blades of the Shogun (port) | Switch 2

The Cube, Save Us | PC

Thursday, March 19

The Coin Game (1.0 release) | PS5, Xbox Series X/S, PC

Crimson Desert | PS5, Xbox Series X/S, PC

Death Stranding 2: On the Beach (port) | PC

Dragonkin: The Banished (1.0 release) | PS5, Xbox Series X/S, PC

Rushing Beat X: Return of Brawl Brothers | PS5, Xbox Series X/S, Switch, PC

Voidwrought (port) | PS5, Xbox Series X/S

Friday, March 20

Ghost Master: Resurrection | PS5, Xbox Series X/S, Switch, PC

Crystalfall | PC

The Week In Games is Kotaku’s weekly roundup of notable, interesting, and/or big video game launches that are happening over the next seven days. Feel free to share other game release dates that might not be included above in the comments below.



https://kotaku.com/march-16-2026-every-game-coming-out-crimson-desert-2000679214
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Cut Speeches, Ties, And Snubs: All This Year’s Oscars Drama
item.content:encoded:
It’s simply not awards season without a little bit of drama. If you put a bunch of actors and producers in a room, get them dressed up in their finest outfits, and award them for their accomplishments, something unexpected is bound to happen. Add onto this that stans are watching and ready to turn any perceived slight or snub into a viral moment, and events like the Academy Awards end up as likely to generate fan anger as they are celebration. 

This year’s ceremony didn’t have anything quite as dramatic as Will Smith slapping Chris Rock on stage in 2022, but there were a few moments that caught viewers’ attention.

KPop Demon Hunters winners cut off

One moment that managed to ruffle some feathers was when the recipients of Best Original Song got cut off during their acceptance speech. KPop Demon Hunters took the award for “Golden,” and after singer and songwriter Ejae passed the mic over to co-writer Yu Han Lee, he was abruptly played off.

@itsthespotlight

Viewers watching from home were shocked that the songwriters of Golden were cut-off during their acceptance speech for Best Original Song. ‘Alright f*** the Oscars for cutting off Best Original Song for Golden KPop Demon Hunters. Absolute disrespect and disgraceful to the creatives behind the biggest song of the year,’ one user wrote on X. Ejae, Mark Sonnenblick, Joong Gyu Kwak, Yu Han Lee, Hee Dong Nam, Jeong Hoon Seo and Teddy Park won the Oscar for Best Original Song for Golden from KPop Demon Hunters. #kpopdemonhunter #song #oscars #shock

♬ original sound – The Spotlight

KPop Demon Hunters fans have called this disrespectful. The Oscars famously plays off award recipients who go over their allotted time, but the crew had only been on stage for about a minute before getting cut off, and other winners seemed to be granted more time to make comments and offer thank-yous..

In a post-show interview with Variety, Walt Disney Television’s Rob Mills said that event organizers will be assessing how it handles speeches in the future.

“One thing, as we post mortem for next year, will be to look at how we’re handling speeches,” Mills told Variety. “You win the Oscar, you know you go on stage, it could be one person, it could be five or six. Immediately you’ll see the sort of allotted time we have for them. Do we need to look at it and say, okay, designate one person to speak. Maybe you continue it backstage, and we have a feed on social or something like that. We look at everything and figure out what is the most elegant solution, because it is difficult, especially when you’re cutting somebody off and it’s their one moment. We talk about it at the award luncheon, that you have this designated time to speak, and it’s difficult. I don’t know what the most elegant solution is, but it’s obviously something we should look really, really long and hard at.”

The Timothée Chalamet of it all

If you’ve got a healthy relationship with your phone and aren’t always online, you might not be aware that Marty Supreme star Timothée Chalamet has been under scrutiny for comments he made about how “no one cares” about ballet and opera in a lengthy conversation with fellow actor Matthew McConaughey last month. His remarks were deemed flippant by supporters of the classical arts, while others felt like he was, perhaps ineloquently, trying to speak about entertainers going where the audience is. Oscars host Conan O’Brien made a reference to the controversy in his opening speech, joking that security was “extremely tight” this year because there were “concerns about attacks from both the opera and ballet communities.” The camera panned over to Chalamet, who seemed unbothered as he had a big ol’ grin on his face. 

The Oscars’ first tie in years

One of the more surprising developments of the night was that the Oscars awarded its first tie since 2013, with both The Singers and Two People Exchanging Saliva winning the award for best live-action short film thanks to an even split of votes from the jury. Actor Kumail Nanjiani presented the award and brought attention to how irregular it was by riffing on it and telling the audience to “calm down” and that they’d get through it together. After The Singer’s crew accepted their award, Nanjiani joked that it was “ironic” that the Short Film award was going to take twice as long as the others.

There have only been seven ties in all of Oscars history, with the last one being in 2013 when Skyfall and Zero Dark Thirty split the vote for Best Sound Editing.

As always, there were perceived snubs

Any awards season gets people riled up, as everyone has their favorites. One Battle After Another took home the Best Picture award, much to the chagrin of folks who were pulling for Sinners to get the crown. Director Paul Thomas Anderson also won the Best Directing award over Ryan Coogler, and “Golden” winning Best Original Song over “I Lied to You” from Sinners left a bad taste in the mouths of those pulling for Coogler’s vampire flick to take home some of the night’s biggest honors, with the film having broken the record for most nominations in Oscar history with 16 total. That said, Sinners took home four awards, including Michael B. Jordan winning Best Actor for his dual role as brothers Smoke and Stack. Coogler, meanwhile, took  Best Original Screenplay, with the film also winning Best Original Score and Best Cinematography. So even if Sinners didn’t win Best Picture, there’s still a lot for the film’s crew to celebrate.

For a full list of the award show’s winners and nominees, check out the Oscars’ website.



https://kotaku.com/oscars-academy-awards-tie-kpop-demon-hunters-sinners-2000679228
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Marathon Is Embracing The Jank And Adding One Of The Most Requested Features For A Limited Time
item.content:encoded:
Players beg for it. Developers shake their heads. But eventually it gets added. Marathon is the latest multiplayer game that’s getting a duos mode following tons of requests. It’s only coming for a limited time, but I suspect if all goes well, it might stick around for the long haul.

“Alright, everyone, we’re going a bit rogue here,” posted game director Joe Ziegler on Monday afternoon. “We have a bit of an experimental feature that’s in development, we want to test, and we’d love for you to help. As of Wednesday morning 3/18 at 10am PT, we’ll be enabling a limited time duos queue on Perimeter only to test how the game plays with crews of 2 at a wider scale.”

The plan is to run the test for two weeks. There won’t be matchmaking so players can only load into duos mode via pre-made groups. They’ll then be matched only with other duos squads. This feature clearly wasn’t originally planned for launch and is coming in hot. The UX for it is in flux and Ziegler admits “things will be a bit jank.”

“This is a bit of an experimental feature, so we expect to learn some things along the way and make changes to future versions of this,” Ziegler added. “This likely won’t be the final implementation of how it would look and flow in the game, but for now it was easiest way for us to get it to you all to help us test.”

He confirmed that the goal is to make duos a permanent queue option, presumably across other maps as well, if things go well over the next couple of weeks. Keeping the number of players constant across modes will mean ramping up the number of teams overall for duos, which could lead to some unexpected interactions. Maps might be cleared out of loot and enemies faster, or firefights might be so frequent that it’s even more frenetic carnage than usual.

It’ll also be interesting to see whether the matchmaking times are reasonable, considering this could potentially split the existing big but not giant player base even more. It’s not the last test Bungie plans to run like this, so it sounds like we’ll be getting experiments with other new features and tweaks as well.

Marathon has a decent audience on Steam, but it isn’t anywhere near as big as a blockbuster first-party Sony game might need to be in order to remain sustainable over the long term. It’s cool to see the team iterating this quickly just a week after launch. Cryo, the endgame map, is slated to arrive later this week.



https://kotaku.com/marathon-duos-mode-matchmaking-update-2000679224
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: New Nvidia DLSS Tech Gives Characters AI Slop Faces
item.content:encoded:
Today, GPU maker and tech giant Nvidia revealed DLSS 5, a new version of its existing upscaling technology that, based on the first images shared by the company, will slap a nice coat of AI slop onto in-game faces.

On March 16, Nvidia announced the next evolution of DLSS, calling DLSS 5 the company’s “most significant breakthrough in computer graphics since the debut of real-time ray tracing in 2018.” Nvidia’s CEO Jensen Huang called the new tech the “GPT moment” for video game graphics, saying that it blends “hand-crafted rendering with generative AI to deliver a dramatic leap in visual realism while preserving the control artists need for creative expression.” And uh, I have some doubts about that last point. Or really everything.

In the blog post announcing DLSS 5, Nvidia showed off numerous examples, and all of the DLSS 5-enhanced screenshots of Grace in Resident Evil Requiem look like the kind of crap that gets made by angry fans online when they think a woman has too big a chin.

According to Nvidia, here’s how DLSS 5 works (and also why it looks so bad).

DLSS 5 takes a game’s color and motion vectors for each frame as input, and uses an AI model to infuse the scene with photoreal lighting and materials that are anchored to source 3D content and consistent from frame to frame. DLSS 5 runs in real time at up to 4K resolution for smooth, interactive gameplay.

Basically, as described by Nvidia, each frame of the game is being covered in real-time with AI junk that is trying to mimic realism but ends up just creating a really off-putting image that also doesn’t look at all like the original game. Previously, DLSS tech helped games run better by letting the PC render the game at a lower internal res and using AI to upscale it to something better-looking. The results were often impressive. But this…this is something else, and it makes it look like someone ran Resident Evil Requiem through some cheap photo filter.

And it wasn’t just Resident Evil that got slopped up. Nvidia also applied DLSS 5 to Starfield to try to improve that game’s admittedly rough-looking faces. The end result:

Yikes! Other games that Nvivia “enhanced” with DLSS 5 included Hogwarts Legacy and EA Sports FC. The results also look like AI slop! Look out for that older woman in the Hogwarts video who looks especially rough after being improved with AI and DLSS 5. And also notice how little footage Nvidia shows of the games in motion using DLSS 5. I think that’s because it looks so unnatural and odd in motion.

Nvidia says DLSS 5 will be available later this fall in a list of titles including AION 2, Assassin’s Creed Shadows, Black State, CINDER CITY, Delta Force, Hogwarts Legacy, Justice, NARAKA: BLADEPOINT, NTE: Neverness to Everness, Phantom Blade Zero, Resident Evil Requiem, Sea of Remnants, Starfield, The Elder Scrolls IV: Oblivion Remastered, Where Winds Meet, and “more.”

“Bethesda has such a rich history pushing graphics with NVIDIA, going all the way back to Morrowind, with that incredible water,” said Bethesda game director Todd Howard via a statement shared by Nvidia. “When NVIDIA showed us DLSS 5, and we got it running in Starfield, it was amazing how it brought it to life. We’ve played it. We can’t wait for all of you to do so as well.”

I’m probably going to pass on that one, Todd. But thanks anyway.

The internet doesn’t like DLSS 5

As you’d expect, the internet has reacted poorly to the reveal of DLSS 5 and its AI slop faces, with many people pointing out how drastically altered some of the images are from the original source material and questioning who would want this.



https://kotaku.com/new-nvidia-dlss-tech-gives-characters-ai-slop-faces-2000679199
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Geoff Keighley Says Oscars CEO Blocked Him On Instagram After He Criticized Omission Of His Father From In Memoriam Segment
item.content:encoded:
This year’s Academy Awards, as is tradition, featured a segment dedicated to actors, directors, screenwriters, film industry executives and luminaries who had died since the previous year’s ceremony. And the omission of David Keighley, who died last year, has led to his son, Geoff Keighley, publicly calling out the Oscars online.

On Sunday, the 98th Academy Awards were held in Los Angeles. As has been the case for decades now, the Oscars featured an “In Memoriam” segment dedicated to filmmakers, crew members, and actors who passed away over the previous year. And Game Awards creator and host Geoff Keighley was “disappointed” that this year’s segment didn’t include his father, who has been working on IMAX movies since the 1970s.

So incredibly disappointed and heartbroken that the #Oscars and AMPAS chose not to include my father, David Keighley, and his immeasurable contributions to IMAX and cinema in the In Memoriam segment in the broadcast.

I will never forget. pic.twitter.com/bMmICAPn3w

— Geoff Keighley (@geoffkeighley) March 16, 2026

In the 15 years before his death in September 2025, David held the role of IMAX’s first Chief Quality Officer and personally oversaw every IMAX film print for decades before that.  Multiple Hollywood directors, including James Cameron, Christopher Nolan, Denis Villeneuve, and Ryan Coogler, shared statements praising David’s work upon his passing, with Coogler saying, “Anyone who’s ever had their mind blown by the images in an IMAX theatrical presentation has David to thank for it.” Yet despite his contributions to film, David Keighley was not featured in the Oscars’ “In Memoriam” segment.

“So incredibly disappointed and heartbroken that the #Oscars and AMPAS chose not to include my father, David Keighley, and his immeasurable contributions to IMAX and cinema in the In Memoriam segment in the broadcast,” posted Geoff Keighley shortly after the awards aired on March 15. “I will never forget.”

On March 16, Keighley offered an update on the situation, claiming he had been blocked on social media after publicly complaining.

“The Oscars’ response? AMPAS/Oscar CEO Bill Kramer apparently blocked me on Instagram last night after seeing my post expressing disappointment about the broadcast’s In Memoriam omission of my father,” posted Geoff on Twitter. “You truly can’t make this stuff up.”

Geoff and his father’s history with the Oscars

In the comments below both his initial post and his update, many fans and creators shared gifs and clips of arguably the most famous Game Awards moment in the show’s decade-long history: That time Split Fiction and It Takes Two director Josef Fares shouted “Fuck the Oscars!” on the live broadcast during the 2017 Game Awards.

In a 2022 interview with The Ringer, Geoff talked about growing up around movies and award shows and how that helped shape his desires for games to have their own big event, leading to the creation of the Spike Video Game Awards and later the Game Awards.

“My dad would always talk about the Oscars and how much it meant to him,” he told The Ringer. “I always wondered, ’Well, why don’t games have something that people care about that much? What’s the Oscar equivalent for gaming?’”



https://kotaku.com/geoff-keighley-says-oscars-ceo-blocked-him-on-instagram-after-he-criticized-omission-of-his-father-from-in-memoriam-segment-2000679183
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
